frontmatter.title: Part 5: The Central Limit Theorem — Why Everything Becomes Gaussian

children.value: An Applied Scenario — Smoothing the Vibration Stream

children.label: An Applied Scenario — Smoothing the Vibration Stream

children.value: Back to the running motor. The raw accelerometer stream is jagged — every sample is a noisy snapshot. To get a stable reading you do the obvious thing: take a

children.value: of the smoothed signal’s distribution converges to a clean bell curve — even if the raw samples have a weird, skewed, or bimodal distribution.
The first observation is intuitive (averaging cancels noise). The second is much deeper, and it’s the most useful theorem in all of probability.
The Poisson-to-Normal convergence in Part 4 turned out to be a special case of something far more general: the
if you sum or average many independent random variables, the result is approximately Normal — regardless of what distribution the individual variables came from.
That last part is what makes it powerful. The original variables don’t have to be Gaussian. They don’t even have to be the
distribution. As long as you’re adding up enough independent contributions of comparable size, the answer is a bell curve.
This single fact explains an enormous amount:
smooth any signal toward a Gaussian-distributed output
are Gaussian because each reading is a sum of dozens of micro-disturbances
are Gaussian because each measurement aggregates many small sources
are approximately Gaussian around the true gradient — the optimization theory of SGD leans hard on this

children.value: — uniform, exponential, Poisson, Bernoulli, even something bizarre and bimodal. As long as you sum enough of them, the result is Gaussian.
For the moving-average filter on the raw accelerometer stream:

children.value: , and it falls out of the
for free. Want half the noise? Quadruple the window. Want a tenth of the noise? Multiply the window by 100.

It’s also why a smoothed signal looks Gaussian-distributed even when the raw stream is wildly non-Gaussian (impulsive transients, clipped values, mixed regimes). Averaging always pulls you toward the bell.

children.label: CLT Explains the Poisson → Normal Convergence

children.value: . The Poisson-to-Normal convergence from Part 4 is just one instance of the same theorem.

This is not just mathematical elegance — it’s why a single Gaussian noise model works for any sensor at moderate-to-high signal levels, regardless of whether the underlying physics is photon counting, packet arrivals, or something else.
A Sensor Reading is Already a Sum

children.label: A Sensor Reading is Already a Sum

children.value: A single reading from almost any sensor is itself a sum of many independent contributions:

\text{reading} = \underbrace{\text{primary signal}}_{\text{the thing you want}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i} \eta_i}_{\text{thermal / electronic noise}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j} d_j}_{\text{interference / drift}} + \underbrace{q}_{\text{quantization}}
children.html: reading=primary signal⏟the thing you want+∑iηi⏟thermal / electronic noise+∑jdj⏟interference / drift+q⏟quantization\text{reading} = \underbrace{\text{primary signal}}_{\text{the thing you want}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i} \eta_i}_{\text{thermal / electronic noise}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j} d_j}_{\text{interference / drift}} + \underbrace{q}_{\text{quantization}}reading=the thing you wantprimary signal​​+thermal / electronic noisei∑​ηi​​​+interference / driftj∑​dj​​​+quantizationq​​
ηi\eta_iηi​
djd_jdj​

children.value: is itself a sum of many micro-disturbances. By the
, the total error is approximately Gaussian even though the individual sources have completely different distributions.
This is the deeper reason every classical signal-processing tool — Wiener filtering, Kalman filtering, Gaussian denoising, least-squares estimation — assumes Gaussian noise. The
guarantees it for any reading that aggregates enough small independent error sources.
The convergence to Normal is fast for some distributions and slow for others. The rate depends on the
(symmetric): converges fastest — already symmetric, just needs smoothing
(two peaks): slowest — two peaks need the most averaging to merge

children.html: O(1/n)O(1/\sqrt{n})O(1/n​)

children.value: . In practical terms, this tells you how long a moving-average window needs to be before the smoothed output is genuinely Gaussian, or how many frames to average before a background-subtraction model based on a Gaussian assumption is valid.
What to look for in the KS convergence plot:
all curves decrease, but at different rates. The Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) statistic measures the maximum difference between the empirical
. A KS distance below ~0.02 means the distribution is effectively Gaussian for any downstream tool that assumes it.
